title: Rosemary digestif tea
subtitle: A wind-easing herb infusion after a rich meal
excerpt: A simple infusion of dried rosemary leaf, traditionally taken after a heavy or fatty meal to ease sluggish digestion. Short steep, covered, to retain the aromatic oil.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, the early 20th-century botanical writer,, A Modern Herbal, rosemary entry: the digestive, hepatic, and circulatory tradition. Nicholas Culpeper (the 17th-century herbalist), The English Physician, rosemary entry: the warming digestive use. the herbal reference on Rosmarini folium, the traditional use for mild digestive complaints.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried rosemary stores 12 months in a sealed tin away from light and heat. Prepare each cup fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that eases trapped wind and spasm in the digestive tract. Carminative action is largely delivered through volatile oils, which is why steep-covered-with-lid is essential.

content.text: Rosemary (Salvia rosmarinus) is better known as a kitchen herb. Its digestive use is well documented in European herbal tradition. Maud Grieve, the early 20th-century botanical writer, records it as a digestive tonic, particularly useful after a rich or fatty meal. The

action comes from the aromatic oil (rosmarinic acid, cineole, borneol). The infusion must be steeped covered to keep that oil in the cup rather than losing it as steam. A teaspoon of dried rosemary leaf (or a fresh sprig) steeped covered in just-off-boil water for 8 minutes makes a pleasantly aromatic, slightly bitter cup.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Pregnancy. Culinary rosemary in cooking is safe and normal throughout pregnancy. Therapeutic-dose infusions (more than a culinary sprig, drunk daily over weeks) are outside the culinary safe-use context; consult your midwife or herbalist before therapeutic use in pregnancy.

Rosemary essential oil is not for internal use and is not part of this preparation.

Paediatric use. A cup of rosemary tea at this dose is not harmful to older children, but as a therapeutic preparation it is not appropriate for children under twelve without herbalist advice.

items.prepNote: dried leaf or one fresh sprig (about 10-12 cm); dried has a more concentrated volatile oil

content.text: Place the rosemary in a teapot or covered cup. Pour over the hot water. Put the lid on the teapot or cover the cup with a saucer. Steep for 8 minutes. The lid is not optional: rosemary's digestive action sits largely in its aromatic oil, which evaporates readily with steam. After 8 minutes, the infusion should smell strongly aromatic and taste pleasantly bitter and herbal. Strain into a cup and drink warm.

One cup after the main meal, up to three times a day. Take for up to 4 weeks at a time. If digestive symptoms persist beyond 2 weeks, see your doctor. Paediatric use: not as a therapeutic preparation for children under twelve.
Prepare each cup fresh. Dried rosemary stores 12 months in an airtight container, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy

Persistent indigestion needs medical assessment. So does indigestion with weight loss, difficulty swallowing, or blood in the stool. Rosemary tea is for occasional discomfort after a rich meal, not for recurring symptoms.
